title: Das tapfere Schneiderlein
about: The twentieth tale (KHM 20): a little tailor kills seven flies at one blow, stitches himself a belt reading “seven at one blow,” and on the strength of that boast out-tricks giants, a king, and a unicorn to win a kingdom. Spelling follows the 1857 edition (daß, gieng, ward, Thür).
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 20. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.

sentences.t: An einem Sommermorgen saß ein Schneiderlein auf seinem Tisch am Fenster, war guter Dinge und nähte aus Leibeskräften.
sentences.l: On a summer morning a little tailor sat upon his table at the window, was of good cheer, and sewed with all his might.
sentences.i: One summer morning a little tailor sat on his table by the window, in fine spirits, and sewed with all his might.
sentences.n: „guter Dinge“ is a fixed idiom, ‘in good spirits, of good cheer’ (literally ‘of good things,’ genitive). „aus Leibeskräften“ = with all one’s bodily strength — you’ll meet these frozen genitive/plural phrases throughout Grimm.

sentences.t: Da kam eine Bauersfrau die Straße herab und rief „gut Mus feil! gut Mus feil!“
sentences.l: There came a peasant woman down the street and cried, „good jam for sale! good jam for sale!“
sentences.i: Then a peasant woman came down the street, crying, „Good jam for sale! Good jam for sale!“
sentences.n: „feil“ is an archaic predicate adjective, ‘for sale, to be had.’ „Mus“ is a thick fruit purée or jam. The uninflected „gut Mus“ (for gutes Mus) is old colloquial hawker’s speech.

sentences.t: Das klang dem Schneiderlein lieblich in die Ohren, er steckte sein zartes Haupt zum Fenster hinaus und rief „hier herauf, liebe Frau, hier wird sie ihre Waare los.“
sentences.l: That rang lovelily in the little tailor’s ears; he stuck his tender head out of the window and cried, „up here, dear woman, here you’ll get rid of your wares.“
sentences.i: That sounded sweet in the little tailor’s ears; he poked his delicate head out of the window and called, „Up here, good woman — here you’ll be rid of your wares.“
sentences.n: „dem Schneiderlein ... in die Ohren“ — a dative of the person affected: it rang ‘to him’ in the ears. „sie“ here is the old polite third-person ‘you’ (= Sie); „ihre Waare los werden“ = to get rid of your goods. „Waare“ is the 1857 spelling of Ware.

sentences.t: Die Frau stieg die drei Treppen mit ihrem schweren Korbe zu dem Schneider herauf und mußte die Töpfe sämmtlich vor ihm auspacken.
sentences.l: The woman climbed the three flights of stairs with her heavy basket up to the tailor and had to unpack all the pots before him.
sentences.i: The woman climbed the three flights of stairs with her heavy basket up to the tailor, and had to unpack every one of her pots in front of him.
sentences.n: „mußte“ is the 1857 spelling of musste. „sämmtlich“ (modern sämtlich) = all, the whole lot; here adverbial, ‘the pots one and all.’ „Korbe“ has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Er besah sie alle, hob sie in die Höhe, hielt die Nase dran und sagte endlich „das Mus scheint mir gut, wieg sie mir doch vier Loth ab, liebe Frau, wenns auch ein Viertelpfund ist, kommt es mir nicht darauf an.“
sentences.l: He looked them all over, lifted them up on high, held his nose to them, and said at last, „the jam seems good to me; do weigh me out four lot of it, dear woman — and if it’s a quarter-pound too, that makes no difference to me.“
sentences.i: He examined them all, held them up, put his nose to them, and finally said, „The jam looks good to me; weigh me out four lot of it, good woman — and if it comes to a quarter-pound, no matter.“
sentences.n: „wieg ... ab“ is the separable verb abwiegen (‘to weigh out’), imperative. „Loth“ (modern Lot) is an old weight of about 15 grams. „es kommt mir nicht darauf an“ is an idiom, ‘it doesn’t matter to me.’ „wenns“ = wenn es.

sentences.t: Die Frau, welche gehofft hatte einen guten Absatz zu finden, gab ihm was er verlangte, gieng aber ganz ärgerlich und brummig fort.
sentences.l: The woman, who had hoped to find a good sale, gave him what he asked, but went off quite vexed and grumbling.
sentences.i: The woman, who had hoped to find a good sale, gave him what he asked for, but went off quite annoyed and grumbling.
sentences.n: „gieng“ is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling of gehen recurs throughout the edition (gieng, giengen, fieng, fieng an). „ging ... fort“ (fortgehen, ‘to go away’) is separable — the prefix fort jumps to the end.

sentences.t: „Nun, das Mus soll mir Gott gesegnen,“ rief das Schneiderlein, „und soll mir Kraft und Stärke geben,“ holte das Brot aus dem Schrank, schnitt sich ein Stück über den ganzen Laib und strich das Mus darüber.
sentences.l: „Now, may God bless this jam for me,“ cried the little tailor, „and give me strength and vigour,“ fetched the bread from the cupboard, cut himself a slice across the whole loaf, and spread the jam over it.
sentences.i: „Now, may God bless this jam for me,“ cried the little tailor, „and give me strength and vigour!“ — and he fetched the bread from the cupboard, cut himself a slice right across the loaf, and spread the jam on it.
sentences.n: „soll mir Gott gesegnen“ — soll here expresses a wish, ‘may God bless.’ „mir“ is a dative of interest, ‘bless it for me.’ „schnitt sich ein Stück“ — the reflexive dative sich, ‘cut himself a piece.’

sentences.t: „Das wird nicht bitter schmecken,“ sprach er, „aber erst will ich den Wams fertig machen, eh ich anbeiße.“
sentences.l: „That will not taste bitter,“ he said, „but first I will finish the jerkin before I bite in.“
sentences.i: „That won’t taste bitter,“ he said, „but first I’ll finish the jerkin before I take a bite.“
sentences.n: „Wams“ is a doublet or jerkin, a close-fitting jacket. „eh“ is a colloquial/archaic form of ehe (‘before’). „anbeiße“ is from the separable anbeißen (‘to bite into’).

sentences.t: Er legte das Brot neben sich, nähte weiter und machte vor Freude immer größere Stiche.
sentences.l: He laid the bread beside him, sewed on, and out of joy made ever larger stitches.
sentences.i: He laid the bread beside him, went on sewing, and in his joy made bigger and bigger stitches.
sentences.n: „vor Freude“ — vor + dative gives a cause, ‘out of / for joy.’ „immer größere“ = ever larger; immer + comparative marks a steady increase.

sentences.t: Indeß stieg der Geruch von dem süßen Mus hinauf an die Wand, wo die Fliegen in großer Menge saßen, so daß sie heran gelockt wurden und sich scharenweiß darauf nieder ließen.
sentences.l: Meanwhile the smell of the sweet jam rose up along the wall, where the flies sat in great number, so that they were lured over and settled down upon it in droves.
sentences.i: Meanwhile the smell of the sweet jam rose up the wall, where flies sat in great numbers, so that they were drawn over and settled on it in swarms.
sentences.n: „Indeß“ is the 1857 spelling of indes (‘meanwhile’). „daß“ is the 1857 spelling of dass, kept throughout this edition. „ließen sich ... nieder“ is the separable niederlassen (‘to settle, alight’); „scharenweiß“ (modern scharenweise) = in droves.

sentences.t: „Ei, wer hat euch eingeladen?“ sprach das Schneiderlein, und jagte die ungebetenen Gäste fort.
sentences.l: „Why, who invited you?“ said the little tailor, and drove the uninvited guests away.
sentences.i: „Hey, who invited you?“ said the little tailor, and chased the uninvited guests away.
sentences.n: „jagte ... fort“ is the separable fortjagen (‘to chase away’). „ungebeten“ = uninvited, the negated past participle of bitten (‘to ask’).

sentences.t: Die Fliegen aber, die kein deutsch verstanden, ließen sich nicht abweisen, sondern kamen in immer größerer Gesellschaft wieder.
sentences.l: But the flies, which understood no German, would not be turned away, but came again in ever greater company.
sentences.i: But the flies, which understood no German, would not be put off, and came back in ever greater numbers.
sentences.n: „ließen sich nicht abweisen“ — lassen + infinitive as a passive-like ‘would not let themselves be turned away.’ „sondern“ (‘but rather’) follows the negation nicht, as against a plain aber.

sentences.t: Da lief dem Schneiderlein endlich, wie man sagt, die Laus über die Leber, es langte aus seiner Hölle nach einem Tuchlappen, und „wart, ich will es euch geben!“ schlug es unbarmherzig drauf.
sentences.l: Then at last, as the saying goes, the louse ran over the little tailor’s liver; it reached out of its ‘hell’ for a cloth-rag, and — „wait, I’ll give it to you!“ — struck mercilessly upon them.
sentences.i: Then at last, as they say, the little tailor lost his temper; he reached into his scrap-drawer for a cloth-rag and — „just wait, I’ll give it to you!“ — struck down among them without mercy.
sentences.n: „die Laus über die Leber laufen“ is a set idiom, ‘to get annoyed, lose one’s temper.’ „Hölle“ (literally ‘hell’) was a tailor’s word for the drawer or bag under the table where scraps fell. „drauf“ = darauf, ‘upon it/them.’

sentences.t: Als es abzog und zählte, so lagen nicht weniger als sieben vor ihm todt und streckten die Beine.
sentences.l: When it drew off and counted, there lay no fewer than seven before it dead, and stretched out their legs.
sentences.i: When he drew back and counted, no fewer than seven lay dead before him with their legs stretched out.
sentences.n: „es“ refers to das Schneiderlein (neuter), so grammatically ‘it’ — read as ‘he.’ „die Beine strecken“ literally ‘to stretch out the legs,’ i.e. to lie dead. „abzog“ is from separable abziehen (‘to draw off, withdraw’).

sentences.t: „Bist du so ein Kerl?“ sprach er, und mußte selbst seine Tapferkeit bewundern, „das soll die ganze Stadt erfahren.“
sentences.l: „Are you such a fellow?“ he said, and had himself to admire his own bravery, „the whole town shall learn of this.“
sentences.i: „So you’re that kind of fellow?“ he said, and could not help admiring his own bravery. „The whole town shall hear of this.“
sentences.n: „das soll die ganze Stadt erfahren“ — erfahren = to learn, find out; soll expresses ‘is to, shall.’ „selbst“ = ‘himself, even’ (he admired his valour himself).

sentences.t: Und in der Hast schnitt sich das Schneiderlein einen Gürtel, nähte ihn und stickte mit großen Buchstaben darauf: „siebene auf einen Streich!“
sentences.l: And in haste the little tailor cut himself a belt, sewed it, and embroidered upon it in large letters: „seven at one blow!“
sentences.i: And in his haste the little tailor cut himself a belt, sewed it, and embroidered on it in large letters: „Seven at one blow!“
sentences.n: „siebene“ is an old, emphatic form of sieben (‘seven’). This boast, „siebene auf einen Streich,“ becomes the tale’s refrain — the double meaning (seven flies, but everyone hears seven men) drives the whole plot.

sentences.t: „Ei was Stadt!“ sprach er weiter, „die ganze Welt solls erfahren!“ und sein Herz wackelte ihm vor Freude wie ein Lämmerschwänzchen.
sentences.l: „Why, what — town!“ he went on, „the whole world shall learn of it!“ and his heart wagged in him for joy like a little lamb’s tail.
sentences.i: „The town — pah!“ he went on, „the whole world shall hear of it!“ and his heart wagged with joy like a little lamb’s tail.
sentences.n: „Ei was Stadt!“ is a dismissive exclamation, roughly ‘the town? never mind the town!’ „solls“ = soll es. „wackelte ihm ... das Herz“ uses a dative of the person (ihm), ‘his heart wagged’ — a dative of possession common in German where English uses a possessive.

sentences.t: Der Schneider band sich den Gürtel um den Leib, und wollte in die Welt hinaus, weil er meinte die Werkstätte sei zu klein für seine Tapferkeit.
sentences.l: The tailor bound the belt about his waist and wanted out into the world, because he thought his workshop was too small for his bravery.
sentences.i: The tailor tied the belt round his waist and wanted to go out into the world, for he thought his workshop too small for his bravery.
sentences.n: „sei“ is subjunctive I in reported thought (‘he thought it *was*’) — this reported-speech subjunctive turns up constantly in Grimm. „wollte in die Welt hinaus“ — the verb of motion (gehen) is dropped after the modal wollte.

sentences.t: Eh er abzog, suchte er im Haus herum ob nichts da wäre, was er mitnehmen könnte, er fand aber nichts als einen alten Käs, den steckte er ein.
sentences.l: Ere he set off, he searched about the house whether nothing were there that he could take along; but he found nothing except an old cheese, which he pocketed.
sentences.i: Before setting off, he searched about the house to see whether there was anything he could take along, but he found nothing but an old cheese, which he pocketed.
sentences.n: „wäre ... könnte“ are subjunctive II, marking the doubtful ‘whether there might be anything.’ „nichts als“ = nothing but. „steckte ... ein“ is the separable einstecken (‘to pocket’); „den“ is a demonstrative fronted for emphasis, ‘that one he pocketed.’

sentences.t: Vor dem Thore bemerkte er einen Vogel, der sich im Gesträuch gefangen hatte, der mußte zu dem Käse in die Tasche.
sentences.l: Before the gate he noticed a bird that had caught itself in the bushes; that one had to go, to the cheese, into the pocket.
sentences.i: Before the gate he spied a bird that had got caught in the bushes; it had to join the cheese in his pocket.
sentences.n: „Thore“ is the 1857 spelling of Tore (‘gate’), with the old -th-; the same -th- returns in Thür, Thron, thun. „der mußte ... in die Tasche“ — the verb of motion is again dropped after the modal (‘had to [go] into the pocket’).

sentences.t: Nun nahm er den Weg tapfer zwischen die Beine, und weil er leicht und behend war, fühlte er keine Müdigkeit.
sentences.l: Now he took the road bravely between his legs, and because he was light and nimble, he felt no weariness.
sentences.i: Now he bravely set off down the road, and being light and nimble, he felt no weariness.
sentences.n: „den Weg zwischen die Beine nehmen“ is a colloquial idiom, ‘to take to the road, get walking.’

sentences.t: Der Weg führte ihn auf einen Berg, und als er den höchsten Gipfel erreicht hatte, so saß da ein gewaltiger Riese und schaute sich ganz gemächlich um.
sentences.l: The road led him up a mountain, and when he had reached the highest summit, there sat a mighty giant and looked about himself quite at his leisure.
sentences.i: The road led him up a mountain, and when he had reached the very top, there sat a huge giant, gazing about him quite at his ease.
sentences.n: „schaute sich ... um“ is the separable umschauen (‘to look around’), reflexive. The „so“ after the als-clause is a resumptive ‘then / there,’ common in this older narration.

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein gieng beherzt auf ihn zu, redete ihn an und sprach „guten Tag, Kamerad, gelt, du sitzest da, und besiehst dir die weitläuftige Welt?
sentences.l: The little tailor went boldly up to him, addressed him, and said, „good day, comrade — so you sit there and look over the wide world for yourself, eh?
sentences.i: The little tailor went boldly up to him, spoke to him, and said, „Good day, comrade — sitting there taking in the wide world, are you?
sentences.n: „gieng ... auf ihn zu“ (zugehen auf, ‘to walk up to’) and „redete ihn an“ (anreden, ‘to address’) are both separable. „gelt“ is a dialect tag, ‘isn’t that so? eh?’ „sitzest“ / „besiehst“ carry the old 2nd-person -st on du. „weitläuftig“ is the 1857 form of weitläufig (‘spacious, extensive’).

sentences.t: ich bin eben auf dem Wege dahin und will mich versuchen.
sentences.l: I am just on the way thither and mean to try myself.
sentences.i: I’m just on my way there, to try my fortune.
sentences.n: „eben“ here = ‘just, precisely.’ „sich versuchen“ = to test oneself, try one’s luck.

sentences.t: Hast du Lust mit zu gehen?“
sentences.l: Have you a mind to come along?“
sentences.n: „Lust haben“ = to feel like, have a mind to. „mit zu gehen“ splits the separable mitgehen (‘to come along’) around the zu.

sentences.t: Der Riese sah den Schneider verächtlich an und sprach „du Lump! du miserabler Kerl!“
sentences.l: The giant looked at the tailor contemptuously and said, „you scoundrel! you miserable fellow!“
sentences.i: The giant looked at the tailor scornfully and said, „You ragamuffin! You wretched little fellow!“
sentences.n: „sah ... an“ is the separable ansehen (‘to look at’).

sentences.t: „Das wäre!“ antwortete das Schneiderlein, knöpfte den Rock auf und zeigte dem Riesen den Gürtel, „da kannst du lesen was ich für ein Mann bin.“
sentences.l: „Is that so!“ answered the little tailor, unbuttoned his coat, and showed the giant the belt, „there you can read what kind of man I am.“
sentences.i: „We’ll see about that!“ answered the little tailor, unbuttoned his coat, and showed the giant the belt: „There you can read what sort of man I am.“
sentences.n: „Das wäre!“ is an elliptical retort in the subjunctive, roughly ‘that would be something! / we’ll see.’ „knöpfte ... auf“ is the separable aufknöpfen. „was ich für ein Mann bin“ — was für ein = ‘what kind of.’

sentences.t: Der Riese las „siebene auf einen Streich,“ meinte das wären Menschen gewesen, die der Schneider erschlagen hätte, und kriegte ein wenig Respekt vor dem kleinen Kerl.
sentences.l: The giant read „seven at one blow,“ thought these had been men whom the tailor had slain, and got a little respect for the small fellow.
sentences.i: The giant read „seven at one blow,“ took it to mean men the tailor had killed, and gained a little respect for the small fellow.
sentences.n: „wären ... gewesen ... hätte erschlagen“ are subjunctive of reported thought — the giant’s (mistaken) supposition. „kriegte“ (from kriegen) is colloquial for ‘got.’

sentences.t: Doch wollte er ihn erst prüfen, nahm einen Stein in die Hand, und drückte ihn zusammen daß das Wasser heraus tropfte.
sentences.l: Yet he wanted to test him first, took a stone in his hand, and pressed it together so that the water dripped out.
sentences.i: Still, he wanted to test him first; he took a stone in his hand and squeezed it so that water dripped out of it.
sentences.n: „drückte ... zusammen“ is the separable zusammendrücken (‘to squeeze together’). The „daß“-clause of result drops its ‘so’: ‘(so) that the water dripped out.’

sentences.t: „Das mach mir nach,“ sprach der Riese, „wenn du Stärke hast.“
sentences.l: „Do that after me,“ said the giant, „if you have strength.“
sentences.i: „Do that if you can,“ said the giant, „if you have any strength.“
sentences.n: „mach ... nach“ is the separable nachmachen (‘to imitate, copy’), imperative; „mir“ is dative, ‘copy it after me.’

sentences.t: „Ists weiter nichts?“ sagte das Schneiderlein, „das ist bei unser einem Spielwerk,“ griff in die Tasche, holte den weichen Käs und drückte ihn daß der Saft heraus lief.
sentences.l: „Is that nothing more?“ said the little tailor, „that’s mere child’s play for the likes of us,“ reached into his pocket, took out the soft cheese, and pressed it so that the juice ran out.
sentences.i: „Is that all?“ said the little tailor. „That’s mere child’s play for the likes of me,“ reached into his pocket, took out the soft cheese, and squeezed it till the whey ran out.
sentences.n: „bei unser einem“ is an old idiom, ‘for one of us, for the likes of us.’ „Spielwerk“ = a trifle, child’s play. The giant thinks it a stone; the joke is the cheese.

sentences.t: „Gelt,“ sprach er, „das war ein wenig besser?“
sentences.l: „Eh,“ he said, „that was a bit better?“
sentences.i: „There now,“ he said, „that was a bit better, wasn’t it?“
sentences.n: „Gelt“ again — the confirmation tag, ‘isn’t that so?’

sentences.t: Der Riese wußte nicht was er sagen sollte, und konnte es von dem Männlein nicht glauben.
sentences.l: The giant knew not what he should say, and could not believe it of the little man.
sentences.i: The giant did not know what to say, and could not believe it of the little man.
sentences.n: „wußte“ is the 1857 spelling of wusste. „Männlein“ is a diminutive, ‘little man.’

sentences.t: Da hob der Riese einen Stein auf und warf ihn so hoch, daß man ihn mit Augen kaum noch sehen konnte: „nun, du Erpelmännchen, das thu mir nach.“
sentences.l: Then the giant picked up a stone and threw it so high that one could scarcely still see it with the eyes: „now, you little drake, do that after me.“
sentences.i: Then the giant picked up a stone and hurled it so high that one could barely see it any more: „Now, you little squirt, do that if you can.“
sentences.n: „hob ... auf“ is the separable aufheben (‘to pick up’). „thu“ is the 1857 spelling of tu (imperative of thun = modern tun). „Erpelmännchen“ (‘little drake’) is a mocking diminutive, roughly ‘little squirt.’

sentences.t: „Gut geworfen,“ sagte der Schneider, „aber der Stein hat doch wieder zur Erde herabfallen müssen, ich will dir einen werfen, der soll gar nicht wieder kommen;“ griff in die Tasche, nahm den Vogel und warf ihn in die Luft.
sentences.l: „Well thrown,“ said the tailor, „but after all the stone had to fall back down to the earth; I’ll throw you one that shall not come back at all;“ reached into his pocket, took the bird, and threw it into the air.
sentences.i: „Well thrown,“ said the tailor, „but the stone had to come down to earth again after all; I’ll throw you one that won’t come back at all,“ reached into his pocket, took the bird, and threw it into the air.
sentences.n: „hat ... herabfallen müssen“ — a double infinitive with the modal müssen at the end (‘has had to fall down’). „herabfallen“ (‘to fall down’) is separable.

sentences.t: Der Vogel, froh über seine Freiheit, stieg auf, flog fort und kam nicht wieder.
sentences.l: The bird, glad of its freedom, rose up, flew off, and came not again.
sentences.i: The bird, glad of its freedom, rose up, flew away, and did not come back.
sentences.n: Three separable verbs in a row: „stieg auf“ (aufsteigen), „flog fort“ (fortfliegen), „kam ... wieder“ (wiederkommen).

sentences.t: „Wie gefällt dir das Stückchen, Kamerad?“ fragte der Schneider.
sentences.l: „How does the little trick please you, comrade?“ asked the tailor.
sentences.i: „How do you like that little trick, comrade?“ asked the tailor.
sentences.n: „gefällt dir“ — gefallen takes the dative person: ‘it pleases *to you*,’ i.e. how do you like it. „Stückchen“ here = a little feat or trick.

sentences.t: „Werfen kannst du wohl,“ sagte der Riese, „aber nun wollen wir sehen ob du im Stande bist etwas ordentliches zu tragen.“
sentences.l: „Throw you can well enough,“ said the giant, „but now we’ll see whether you are in a state to carry something proper.“
sentences.i: „Throwing you can do well enough,“ said the giant, „but now let’s see whether you’re able to carry something decent.“
sentences.n: „Werfen kannst du wohl“ fronts the infinitive for emphasis. „im Stande sein“ (modern imstande) = to be able, capable. „etwas ordentliches“ = something proper / substantial.

sentences.t: Er führte das Schneiderlein zu einem mächtigen Eichbaum, der da gefällt auf dem Boden lag, und sagte „wenn du stark genug bist, so hilf mir den Baum aus dem Walde heraus tragen.“
sentences.l: He led the little tailor to a mighty oak that lay felled there on the ground, and said, „if you are strong enough, then help me carry the tree out of the wood.“
sentences.i: He led the little tailor to a mighty oak that lay felled on the ground, and said, „If you’re strong enough, help me carry the tree out of the forest.“
sentences.n: „gefällt“ is the past participle of fällen (‘to fell’) — not to be confused with gefallen (‘to please’). „heraus tragen“ (heraustragen, ‘to carry out’) is separable; „hilf“ is the imperative of helfen, which takes the dative (mir).

sentences.t: „Gerne,“ antwortete der kleine Mann, „nimm du nur den Stamm auf deine Schulter, ich will die Äste mit dem Gezweig aufheben und tragen, das ist doch das schwerste.“
sentences.l: „Gladly,“ answered the little man, „just you take the trunk on your shoulder; I will lift and carry the boughs with the branchwork — that’s the heaviest, after all.“
sentences.i: „Gladly,“ answered the little man. „You just take the trunk on your shoulder; I’ll lift and carry the boughs and all the branches — that’s the heaviest part, after all.“
sentences.n: „nimm du nur“ — the added du and nur give a coaxing imperative, ‘you just take.’ „Gezweig“ is a collective, ‘the branches, branchwork.’ „das schwerste“ = the heaviest part (his sly trick: he takes the light end).

sentences.t: Der Riese nahm den Stamm auf die Schulter, der Schneider aber setzte sich auf einen Ast, und der Riese, der sich nicht umsehen konnte, mußte den ganzen Baum und das Schneiderlein noch obendrein forttragen.
sentences.l: The giant took the trunk on his shoulder, but the tailor seated himself on a branch, and the giant, who could not look around, had to carry off the whole tree and the little tailor into the bargain.
sentences.i: The giant took the trunk on his shoulder, but the tailor perched himself on a branch, and the giant, who could not look behind him, had to carry off the whole tree and the little tailor on top of it.
sentences.n: „obendrein“ = on top of it, into the bargain. „forttragen“ (‘to carry off’) is separable, here held together after the modal mußte.

sentences.t: Es war da hinten ganz lustig und guter Dinge, pfiff das Liedchen „es ritten drei Schneider zum Thore hinaus,“ als wäre das Baumtragen ein Kinderspiel.
sentences.l: It was quite merry back there and of good cheer, whistled the little song „three tailors rode out of the gate,“ as if tree-carrying were child’s play.
sentences.i: Back there he was quite merry and in fine spirits, whistling the little song „Three tailors rode out at the gate,“ as if carrying trees were child’s play.
sentences.n: „Es“ = the little tailor (neuter Schneiderlein). „guter Dinge“ recurs, ‘in good spirits.’ „als wäre ... ein Kinderspiel“ — subjunctive II after als (‘as if it were’).

sentences.t: Der Riese, nachdem er ein Stück Wegs die schwere Last fortgeschleppt hatte, konnte nicht weiter und rief „hör, ich muß den Baum fallen lassen.“
sentences.l: The giant, after he had dragged the heavy load a stretch of the way, could go no further and cried, „listen, I must let the tree fall.“
sentences.i: The giant, after dragging the heavy load part of the way, could go no further, and cried, „Listen, I have to let the tree drop.“
sentences.n: „ein Stück Wegs“ = a stretch of the road (Wegs is an old genitive). „fortgeschleppt“ is the past participle of fortschleppen (‘to drag off’). „hör“ is the imperative of hören, ‘listen, hark.’

sentences.t: Der Schneider sprang behendiglich herab, faßte den Baum mit beiden Armen, als wenn er ihn getragen hätte, und sprach zum Riesen „du bist ein so großer Kerl und kannst den Baum nicht einmal tragen.“
sentences.l: The tailor sprang down nimbly, grasped the tree with both arms as if he had been carrying it, and said to the giant, „you are such a big fellow and can’t even carry the tree.“
sentences.i: The tailor sprang down nimbly, seized the tree with both arms as if he had been carrying it, and said to the giant, „You’re such a big fellow, and you can’t even carry the tree.“
sentences.n: „behendiglich“ is an archaic adverb, ‘nimbly, quickly.’ „als wenn er ihn getragen hätte“ — subjunctive II, ‘as if he had carried it.’ „nicht einmal“ = not even.

sentences.t: Sie giengen zusammen weiter, und als sie an einem Kirschbaum vorbei kamen, faßte der Riese die Krone des Baums, wo die zeitigsten Früchte hiengen, bog sie herab, gab sie dem Schneider in die Hand und hieß ihn essen.
sentences.l: They went on together, and as they came past a cherry tree, the giant grasped the crown of the tree, where the ripest fruits hung, bent it down, gave it into the tailor’s hand, and bade him eat.
sentences.i: They went on together, and as they passed a cherry tree, the giant seized the crown of the tree, where the ripest fruit hung, bent it down, put it into the tailor’s hand, and bade him eat.
sentences.n: „giengen“ and „hiengen“ carry the 1857 -ie- (modern gingen, hingen). „kamen ... vorbei“ (vorbeikommen, ‘to pass by’) is separable. „hieß ihn essen“ — heißen + bare infinitive = ‘to bid, order (someone to do).’

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein aber war viel zu schwach um den Baum zu halten, und als der Riese los ließ, fuhr der Baum in die Höhe, und der Schneider ward mit in die Luft geschnellt.
sentences.l: But the little tailor was much too weak to hold the tree, and when the giant let go, the tree shot up on high, and the tailor was flung up into the air along with it.
sentences.i: But the little tailor was far too weak to hold the tree, and when the giant let go, the tree sprang up, and the tailor was flung into the air along with it.
sentences.n: „ward“ is the archaic preterite of werden (modern wurde), here forming the passive ‘was flung.’ You’ll meet ward throughout Grimm. „um ... zu halten“ = ‘too weak *to* hold.’ „ließ los“ (loslassen) and „fuhr ... in die Höhe“ are separable.

sentences.t: Als er wieder ohne Schaden herabgefallen war, sprach der Riese „was ist das, hast du nicht Kraft die schwache Gerte zu halten?“
sentences.l: When he had fallen down again without harm, the giant said, „what is this — have you not the strength to hold the weak switch?“
sentences.i: When he had come down again unhurt, the giant said, „What’s this — haven’t you the strength to hold a thin little switch?“
sentences.n: „herabgefallen“ is the past participle of herabfallen (‘to fall down’), separable. „Gerte“ = a thin switch or rod — the giant mockingly calls the whole tree a mere twig.

sentences.t: „An der Kraft fehlt es nicht,“ antwortete das Schneiderlein, „meinst du das wäre etwas für einen, der siebene mit einem Streich getroffen hat?
sentences.l: „It’s not strength that’s lacking,“ answered the little tailor, „do you think that would be anything for one who has struck seven at one blow?
sentences.i: „It isn’t strength that’s wanting,“ answered the little tailor. „Do you suppose that’s anything for a man who has struck seven at one blow?
sentences.n: „An der Kraft fehlt es nicht“ — the impersonal es fehlt an + dative, ‘there is no lack of strength.’ „wäre“ is subjunctive II, the hypothetical ‘would that be.’

sentences.t: ich bin über den Baum gesprungen, weil die Jäger da unten in das Gebüsch schießen.
sentences.l: I jumped over the tree because the huntsmen down there are shooting into the bushes.
sentences.i: I jumped over the tree because the huntsmen down there are shooting into the thicket.
sentences.n: The tailor invents a face-saving reason: he ‘jumped’ (rather than was flung). „Jäger“ can be singular or plural; here plural, ‘the huntsmen.’

sentences.t: Spring nach, wenn dus vermagst.“
sentences.l: Jump after, if you can.“
sentences.i: Jump after me, if you can.“
sentences.n: „Spring nach“ is the separable nachspringen (‘to jump after’), imperative. „dus“ = du es; „vermagst“ is 2nd sg of vermögen (‘to be able’).

sentences.t: Der Riese machte den Versuch, konnte aber nicht über den Baum kommen, sondern blieb in den Ästen hängen, also daß das Schneiderlein auch hier die Oberhand behielt.
sentences.l: The giant made the attempt, but could not get over the tree, and instead stuck fast in the branches, so that the little tailor here too kept the upper hand.
sentences.i: The giant made the attempt, but could not clear the tree, and stuck fast in the branches, so that here too the little tailor kept the upper hand.
sentences.n: „blieb ... hängen“ (hängenbleiben, ‘to get caught, stuck’) is separable. „also daß“ = ‘so that’ (older than the modern so daß). „die Oberhand behalten“ = to keep the upper hand.

sentences.t: Der Riese sprach „wenn du ein so tapferer Kerl bist, so komm mit in unsere Höhle und übernachte bei uns.“
sentences.l: The giant said, „if you are such a brave fellow, then come along into our cave and spend the night with us.“
sentences.i: The giant said, „If you’re such a brave fellow, then come along to our cave and stay the night with us.“
sentences.n: „komm mit“ (mitkommen) and „übernachte“ are imperatives; the wenn ... so frame is ‘if ..., then ....’

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein war bereit und folgte ihm.
sentences.l: The little tailor was ready and followed him.
sentences.n: „folgte“ (folgen, ‘to follow’) takes the dative, ‘followed him.’

sentences.t: Als sie in der Höhle anlangten, saßen da noch andere Riesen beim Feuer, und jeder hatte ein gebratenes Schaf in der Hand und aß davon.
sentences.l: When they arrived in the cave, there sat still other giants by the fire, and each had a roasted sheep in his hand and ate of it.
sentences.i: When they reached the cave, other giants were sitting by the fire, and each held a roasted sheep in his hand and ate from it.
sentences.n: „anlangten“ (anlangen, ‘to arrive’) is separable. „aß davon“ — davon = ‘of it, from it.’

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein sah sich um und dachte „es ist doch hier viel weitläuftiger als in meiner Werkstatt.“
sentences.l: The little tailor looked around and thought, „it is much roomier here, after all, than in my workshop.“
sentences.n: „sah sich um“ (umsehen) is separable and reflexive. „weitläuftiger“ is the comparative of weitläuftig (1857 for weitläufig), ‘more spacious.’ „doch“ is a flavouring particle, ‘after all.’

sentences.t: Der Riese wies ihm ein Bett an und sagte er sollte sich hineinlegen und ausschlafen.
sentences.l: The giant assigned him a bed and said he should lie down in it and sleep his fill.
sentences.i: The giant showed him a bed and told him to lie down in it and sleep his fill.
sentences.n: „wies ... an“ is the separable anweisen (‘to assign, allot’). „sollte“ in reported speech, ‘he was to.’ „hineinlegen“ (reflexive, ‘to lie down inside’) and „ausschlafen“ (‘to sleep one’s fill’) are separable.

sentences.t: Dem Schneiderlein war aber das Bett zu groß, er legte sich nicht hinein, sondern kroch in eine Ecke.
sentences.l: But to the little tailor the bed was too big; he did not lie down in it, but crept into a corner.
sentences.i: But the bed was too big for the little tailor; he did not lie down in it, but crept into a corner.
sentences.n: „Dem Schneiderlein war das Bett zu groß“ — a dative of the person: ‘to him the bed was too big.’ „legte sich ... hinein“ (sich hineinlegen) is separable; „kroch“ is the preterite of kriechen (‘to crawl, creep’).

sentences.t: Als es Mitternacht war, und der Riese meinte das Schneiderlein läge in tiefem Schlafe, so stand er auf, nahm eine große Eisenstange und schlug das Bett mit einem Schlag durch, und meinte er hätte dem Grashüpfer den Garaus gemacht.
sentences.l: When it was midnight, and the giant thought the little tailor lay in deep sleep, he got up, took a great iron bar, and struck the bed through with one blow, and thought he had done the grasshopper in.
sentences.i: When midnight came, and the giant supposed the little tailor was lying in deep sleep, he got up, took a great iron bar, and struck the bed through with one blow, thinking he had finished the grasshopper off.
sentences.n: „läge“ (subjunctive II of liegen) and „hätte ... gemacht“ mark the giant’s reported/mistaken belief. „schlug ... durch“ (durchschlagen) is separable. „den Garaus machen“ is a set idiom, ‘to finish off, do in’; „Grashüpfer“ (‘grasshopper’) is his contemptuous name for the tailor.

sentences.t: Mit dem frühsten Morgen giengen die Riesen in den Wald und hatten das Schneiderlein ganz vergessen, da kam es auf einmal ganz lustig und verwegen daher geschritten.
sentences.l: With the earliest morning the giants went into the forest and had quite forgotten the little tailor, when all at once it came striding along, quite merry and bold.
sentences.i: At earliest morning the giants went off into the forest, having quite forgotten the little tailor, when all at once he came striding along, merry and bold as you please.
sentences.n: „frühsten“ is a 1857 superlative form of früh, ‘earliest.’ „kam ... daher geschritten“ — kommen + past participle of motion (‘came striding along’), a common Grimm construction. „auf einmal“ = all at once.

sentences.t: Die Riesen erschracken, fürchteten es schlüge sie alle todt und liefen in einer Hast fort.
sentences.l: The giants took fright, feared it would strike them all dead, and ran off in a great hurry.
sentences.i: The giants took fright, feared he would strike them all dead, and ran off in a great hurry.
sentences.n: „erschracken“ is the 1857/archaic preterite of erschrecken (‘took fright’; modern erschraken). „schlüge“ is subjunctive II in reported fear, ‘that he would strike.’ „todt“ is the old spelling of tot (‘dead’); „liefen ... fort“ (fortlaufen) is separable.

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein zog weiter, immer seiner spitzen Nase nach.
sentences.l: The little tailor moved on, always following his pointed nose.
sentences.i: The little tailor journeyed on, always following his pointed nose.
sentences.n: „zog weiter“ (weiterziehen) is separable. „seiner ... Nase nach“ — the postposition nach follows its dative noun (‘after his nose’), i.e. straight ahead wherever it points.

sentences.t: Nachdem es lange gewandert war, kam es in den Hof eines königlichen Palastes, und da es Müdigkeit empfand, so legte es sich ins Gras und schlief ein.
sentences.l: After it had wandered long, it came into the courtyard of a royal palace, and since it felt weariness, it laid itself in the grass and fell asleep.
sentences.i: After wandering a long while, he came into the courtyard of a royal palace, and since he felt weary, he lay down in the grass and fell asleep.
sentences.n: „Nachdem“ = ‘after’ (conjunction). „da“ here is causal, ‘since, because.’ „schlief ein“ (einschlafen, ‘to fall asleep’) is separable.

sentences.t: Während es da lag, kamen die Leute, betrachteten es von allen Seiten und lasen auf dem Gürtel „siebene auf einen Streich.“
sentences.l: While it lay there, the people came, regarded it from all sides, and read on the belt „seven at one blow.“
sentences.i: While he lay there, people came, looked him over from all sides, and read on his belt „seven at one blow.“
sentences.n: „Während“ = ‘while’ (conjunction). „lasen“ is the preterite plural of lesen (‘to read’).

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sprachen sie, „was will der große Kriegsheld hier mitten im Frieden?
sentences.l: „Ah,“ they said, „what does the great war-hero want here in the midst of peace?
sentences.n: „was will ... ?“ — wollen here in its full sense, ‘what does he want / seek.’ „mitten im Frieden“ = in the midst of peacetime.

sentences.t: Das muß ein mächtiger Herr sein.“
sentences.l: That must be a mighty lord.“
sentences.i: He must be a mighty lord.“
sentences.n: „muß“ (1857 spelling of muss) here expresses inference, ‘must be, surely is.’

sentences.t: Sie giengen und meldeten es dem König, und meinten wenn Krieg ausbrechen sollte, wäre das ein wichtiger und nützlicher Mann, den man um keinen Preis fortlassen dürfte.
sentences.l: They went and reported it to the king, and thought that, if war should break out, this would be an important and useful man whom one durst on no account let go.
sentences.i: They went and reported it to the king, and thought that if war should break out, he would be an important and useful man whom they should on no account let go.
sentences.n: „ausbrechen“ (‘to break out’) is separable. „wäre ... dürfte“ are subjunctive of reported opinion. „um keinen Preis“ = at no price, on no account; „fortlassen“ (‘to let go, release’) is separable.

sentences.t: Dem König gefiel der Rath und er schickte einen von seinen Hofleuten an das Schneiderlein ab, der sollte ihm, wenn es aufgewacht wäre, Kriegsdienste anbieten.
sentences.l: The counsel pleased the king, and he sent off one of his courtiers to the little tailor, who was to offer him, when it had awoken, military service.
sentences.i: The counsel pleased the king, and he sent off one of his courtiers to the little tailor, who was to offer him service in war once he had woken.
sentences.n: „Rath“ is the 1857 spelling of Rat (‘counsel, advice’), with the old -th-. „gefiel“ (gefallen) takes the dative: ‘pleased the king.’ „schickte ... ab“ (abschicken, ‘to send off’) is separable. „aufgewacht wäre“ — pluperfect subjunctive in reported speech.

sentences.t: Der Abgesandte blieb bei dem Schläfer stehen, wartete bis er seine Glieder streckte und die Augen aufschlug, und brachte dann seinen Antrag vor.
sentences.l: The envoy stopped beside the sleeper, waited until he stretched his limbs and opened his eyes, and then brought forward his proposal.
sentences.i: The envoy halted beside the sleeper, waited until he stretched his limbs and opened his eyes, and then put his proposal to him.
sentences.n: „blieb ... stehen“ (stehenbleiben, ‘to stop, stand still’), „aufschlug“ (aufschlagen, here ‘to open [the eyes]’), and „brachte ... vor“ (vorbringen, ‘to bring forward, put forward’) are all separable.

sentences.t: „Eben deshalb bin ich hierher gekommen,“ antwortete er, „ich bin bereit in des Königs Dienste zu treten.“
sentences.l: „For that very reason have I come hither,“ he answered, „I am ready to enter the king’s service.“
sentences.i: „That’s the very reason I’ve come here,“ he answered. „I’m ready to enter the king’s service.“
sentences.n: „Eben deshalb“ = for that very reason. „in Dienste treten“ = to enter service; „des Königs“ is a fronted genitive, ‘the king’s.’

sentences.t: Also ward er ehrenvoll empfangen und ihm eine besondere Wohnung angewiesen.
sentences.l: So he was received honourably, and a separate dwelling assigned to him.
sentences.i: So he was received with honour, and given a lodging of his own.
sentences.n: „ward“ again — archaic preterite of werden, forming two passives (‘was received ... [was] assigned’). „Also“ here = ‘thus, so’ (not English ‘also’). „angewiesen“ is the past participle of anweisen (‘to assign’).

sentences.t: Die Kriegsleute aber waren dem Schneiderlein aufgesessen und wünschten es wäre tausend Meilen weit weg.
sentences.l: But the men of war had taken against the little tailor and wished it were a thousand miles away.
sentences.i: But the soldiers had taken a dislike to the little tailor, and wished he were a thousand miles off.
sentences.n: „jemandem aufgesessen sein“ is an idiom, ‘to bear a grudge against, be fed up with someone’ (+ dative). „wäre“ is subjunctive II of the wish, ‘were it away.’

sentences.t: „Was soll daraus werden?“ sprachen sie untereinander, „wenn wir Zank mit ihm kriegen und er haut zu, so fallen auf jeden Streich siebene.
sentences.l: „What is to come of this?“ they said among themselves, „if we get into a quarrel with him and he strikes out, then seven fall at every blow.
sentences.i: „What’s to come of this?“ they said among themselves. „If we get into a quarrel with him and he lays about him, then seven fall at every blow.
sentences.n: „Was soll daraus werden?“ = ‘what is to become of it?’ „haut zu“ (zuhauen, ‘to strike out, lay about’) is separable. „Zank kriegen“ = to get into a quarrel.

sentences.t: Da kann unser einer nicht bestehen.“
sentences.l: There one of us cannot hold his ground.“
sentences.i: Against that, the likes of us can’t hold out.“
sentences.n: „unser einer“ is an old idiom, ‘one of us, the likes of us.’ „bestehen“ here = to hold one’s ground, survive.

sentences.t: Also faßten sie einen Entschluß, begaben sich allesammt zum König und baten um ihren Abschied.
sentences.l: So they took a resolve, betook themselves one and all to the king, and begged for their discharge.
sentences.i: So they came to a decision, went one and all to the king, and asked to be discharged.
sentences.n: „faßten einen Entschluß“ = to come to a decision (Entschluß is the 1857 spelling of Entschluss). „begaben sich“ (sich begeben, ‘to betake oneself, go’). „allesammt“ (modern allesamt) = all together. „um ihren Abschied bitten“ = to ask for one’s discharge.

sentences.t: „Wir sind nicht gemacht,“ sprachen sie, „neben einem Mann auszuhalten, der siebene auf einen Streich schlägt.“
sentences.l: „We are not made,“ they said, „to hold out beside a man who strikes seven at one blow.“
sentences.i: „We’re not made,“ they said, „to serve alongside a man who strikes seven at one blow.“
sentences.n: „auszuhalten“ (aushalten, ‘to hold out, endure’) is separable, here as a zu-infinitive after gemacht.

sentences.t: Der König war traurig daß er um des Einen willen alle seine treuen Diener verlieren sollte, wünschte daß seine Augen ihn nie gesehen hätten und wäre ihn gerne wieder los gewesen.
sentences.l: The king was sad that for the sake of the one he should lose all his faithful servants, wished that his eyes had never seen him, and would gladly have been rid of him again.
sentences.i: The king was grieved that for the sake of this one man he should lose all his faithful servants; he wished his eyes had never seen him, and would gladly have been rid of him.
sentences.n: „um ... willen“ frames a reason, ‘for the sake of’ (here um des Einen willen, ‘for the sake of the one’). „gesehen hätten“ (pluperfect subjunctive) and „wäre ... los gewesen“ (‘would have been rid of’) are hypothetical/wished. „jemanden los sein/werden“ = to be/get rid of someone.

sentences.t: Aber er getrauete sich nicht ihm den Abschied zu geben, weil er fürchtete er möchte ihn sammt seinem Volke todt schlagen und sich auf den königlichen Thron setzen.
sentences.l: But he did not dare to give him his discharge, because he feared he might strike him dead together with his people and seat himself on the royal throne.
sentences.i: But he dared not dismiss him, for he feared the tailor might strike him and all his people dead, and set himself on the royal throne.
sentences.n: „getrauete sich“ is the 1857 form of getraute sich (sich getrauen, ‘to dare’). „möchte“ is subjunctive of reported fear, ‘might.’ „sammt“ (modern samt) = together with (+ dative). „todt schlagen“ (totschlagen, ‘to strike dead’) is separable.

sentences.t: Er sann lange hin und her, endlich fand er einen Rath.
sentences.l: He pondered long this way and that; at last he found a counsel.
sentences.i: He mused long this way and that, and at last hit upon a plan.
sentences.n: „sann“ is the preterite of sinnen (‘to ponder, muse’). „hin und her“ = to and fro, this way and that. „einen Rath finden“ = to find a way out, hit on a plan.

sentences.t: Er schickte zu dem Schneiderlein und ließ ihm sagen weil er ein so großer Kriegsheld wäre, so wollte er ihm ein Anerbieten machen.
sentences.l: He sent to the little tailor and had it said to him that, because he was so great a war-hero, he would make him an offer.
sentences.i: He sent to the little tailor and let him be told that, since he was so great a war-hero, he wished to make him an offer.
sentences.n: „ließ ihm sagen“ — lassen + infinitive, ‘had (a message) said to him.’ „wäre ... wollte“ are reported-speech subjunctives (the king’s message). „Anerbieten“ = an offer, proposal.

sentences.t: In einem Walde seines Landes hausten zwei Riesen, die mit Rauben Morden Sengen und Brennen großen Schaden stifteten: niemand dürfte sich ihnen nahen ohne sich in Lebensgefahr zu setzen.
sentences.l: In a forest of his land there dwelt two giants who, with robbing, murdering, singeing, and burning, wrought great damage: no one durst draw near them without putting himself in mortal danger.
sentences.i: In a forest of his realm dwelt two giants who wrought great havoc with robbing, murder, and fire: no one dared go near them without putting his life in peril.
sentences.n: „hausten“ (hausen) = to dwell, lodge (often of something menacing). The nominalized infinitives „Rauben, Morden, Sengen, Brennen“ are strung without commas as in the original. „dürfte“ = ‘durst, dared’; „sich nahen“ (+ dative) = to approach; „sich in Lebensgefahr setzen“ = to put oneself in mortal danger.

sentences.t: Wenn er diese beiden Riesen überwände und tödtete, so wollte er ihm seine einzige Tochter zur Gemahlin geben und das halbe Königreich zur Ehesteuer; auch sollten hundert Reiter mit ziehen und ihm Beistand leisten.
sentences.l: If he overcame and slew these two giants, then he would give him his only daughter to wife and half the kingdom as marriage-portion; a hundred horsemen too should ride along and lend him aid.
sentences.i: If he overcame and killed these two giants, the king would give him his only daughter in marriage and half the kingdom as her dowry; and a hundred horsemen too should go with him and lend him aid.
sentences.n: „überwände ... tödtete“ (subjunctive II) frame the condition in reported speech; „tödtete“ is the 1857 spelling of tötete. „zur Gemahlin geben“ = to give as a wife; „Ehesteuer“ = marriage-portion, dowry. „mit ziehen“ (mitziehen, ‘to go along’) is separable; „Beistand leisten“ = to lend aid.

sentences.t: „Das wäre so etwas für einen Mann, wie du bist,“ dachte das Schneiderlein, „eine schöne Königstochter und ein halbes Königreich wird einem nicht alle Tage angeboten.“
sentences.l: „That would be just the thing for a man such as you are,“ thought the little tailor, „a fair king’s daughter and half a kingdom are not offered one every day.“
sentences.i: „That would be just the thing for a man like you,“ thought the little tailor, „a fair princess and half a kingdom aren’t offered to you every day.“
sentences.n: „wäre“ subjunctive II, ‘would be.’ „einem“ is the dative of the impersonal man (‘to one’). „wird ... angeboten“ is a passive, ‘is offered’ (anbieten, separable).

sentences.t: „O ja,“ gab er zur Antwort, „die Riesen will ich schon bändigen, und habe die hundert Reiter dabei nicht nöthig: wer siebene auf einen Streich trifft, braucht sich vor zweien nicht zu fürchten.“
sentences.l: „Oh yes,“ he gave in answer, „the giants I’ll tame right enough, and have no need of the hundred horsemen for it: he who strikes seven at one blow need not fear two.“
sentences.i: „Oh yes,“ he answered, „I’ll master the giants well enough, and I’ve no need of the hundred horsemen for that: a man who strikes seven at one blow needn’t be afraid of two.“
sentences.n: „schon“ here is reassuring, ‘right enough, no doubt.’ „nöthig“ is the 1857 spelling of nötig. „sich vor jemandem fürchten“ = to be afraid of someone (+ vor + dative); „zweien“ is the old dative of zwei.

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein zog aus, und die hundert Reiter folgten ihm.
sentences.l: The little tailor set out, and the hundred horsemen followed him.
sentences.n: „zog aus“ (ausziehen, ‘to set out, march off’) is separable. „folgten“ (folgen) takes the dative, ‘followed him.’

sentences.t: Als er zu dem Rand des Waldes kam, sprach er zu seinen Begleitern „bleibt hier nur halten, ich will schon allein mit den Riesen fertig werden.“
sentences.l: When he came to the edge of the forest, he said to his companions, „just stay and halt here; I’ll deal with the giants alone right enough.“
sentences.i: When he reached the edge of the forest, he said to his companions, „Just stay and wait here; I’ll deal with the giants alone, never fear.“
sentences.n: „bleibt ... halten“ — bleiben + infinitive, ‘stay halted.’ „mit jemandem fertig werden“ = to deal with, get the better of someone. „schon“ again reassuring, ‘right enough.’

sentences.t: Dann sprang er in den Wald hinein und schaute sich rechts und links um.
sentences.l: Then he sprang into the forest and looked about him right and left.
sentences.i: Then he sprang into the forest and looked about him to right and left.
sentences.n: „sprang ... hinein“ (hineinspringen) and „schaute sich um“ (umschauen) are both separable; hinein marks motion inward, away from the speaker.

sentences.t: Über ein Weilchen erblickte er beide Riesen: sie lagen unter einem Baume und schliefen und schnarchten dabei, daß sich die Äste auf und nieder bogen.
sentences.l: After a little while he caught sight of both giants: they lay under a tree and slept and snored withal, so that the branches bent up and down.
sentences.i: After a little while he caught sight of the two giants: they lay under a tree, sleeping and snoring so hard that the branches bent up and down.
sentences.n: „Über ein Weilchen“ = after a little while. „dabei“ here = ‘the while, withal.’ The daß-clause is one of result (‘so that ...’); „bogen sich ... auf und nieder“ = bent up and down.

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein, nicht faul, las beide Taschen voll Steine und stieg damit auf den Baum.
sentences.l: The little tailor, not lazy, gathered both pockets full of stones and climbed with them up the tree.
sentences.i: The little tailor, quick as anything, filled both pockets with stones and climbed up the tree with them.
sentences.n: „nicht faul“ is a set phrase, ‘without delay, quick to act.’ „las“ here is lesen in its older sense ‘to gather, pick up’ (as in Ähren lesen, to glean) — not ‘to read.’

sentences.t: Als es in der Mitte war, rutschte es auf einem Ast bis es gerade über die Schläfer zu sitzen kam, und ließ dem einen Riesen einen Stein nach dem andern auf die Brust fallen.
sentences.l: When it was in the middle, it slid along a branch until it came to sit right over the sleepers, and let one stone after another fall onto the one giant’s breast.
sentences.i: When he was halfway up, he slid along a branch until he came to sit right over the sleepers, and let one stone after another drop onto the one giant’s chest.
sentences.n: „zu sitzen kam“ — kommen + zu-infinitive, ‘came to sit.’ „ließ ... fallen“ (fallen lassen, ‘to let fall, drop’). „dem einen Riesen ... auf die Brust“ — dative of the person, ‘onto the one giant’s chest.’

sentences.t: Der Riese spürte lange nichts, doch endlich wachte er auf, stieß seinen Gesellen an und sprach „was schlägst du mich.“
sentences.l: The giant felt nothing for a long while, but at last woke up, nudged his companion, and said, „what are you hitting me for.“
sentences.i: The giant felt nothing for a long while, but at last woke up, nudged his fellow, and said, „What are you hitting me for?“
sentences.n: „wachte ... auf“ (aufwachen) and „stieß ... an“ (anstoßen, ‘to nudge, jab’) are separable. „Geselle“ = fellow, companion.

sentences.t: „Du träumst,“ sagte der andere, „ich schlage dich nicht.“
sentences.l: „You’re dreaming,“ said the other, „I’m not hitting you.“

sentences.t: Sie legten sich wieder zum Schlaf, da warf der Schneider auf den zweiten einen Stein herab.
sentences.l: They lay down again to sleep, whereupon the tailor threw a stone down onto the second one.
sentences.i: They lay down to sleep again, whereupon the tailor threw a stone down onto the second one.
sentences.n: „warf ... herab“ (herabwerfen, ‘to throw down’) is separable. „da“ here = ‘then, whereupon.’

sentences.t: „Was soll das?“ rief der andere, „warum wirfst du mich?“
sentences.l: „What’s this?“ cried the other, „why are you throwing at me?“
sentences.i: „What’s the meaning of this?“ cried the other. „Why are you pelting me?“
sentences.n: „Was soll das?“ = ‘what’s this / what’s that for?’ „wirfst du mich“ — colloquially ‘throw at me,’ i.e. pelt.

sentences.t: „Ich werfe dich nicht,“ antwortete der erste und brummte.
sentences.l: „I’m not throwing at you,“ answered the first, and grumbled.

sentences.t: Sie zankten sich eine Weile herum, doch weil sie müde waren, ließen sies gut sein, und die Augen fielen ihnen wieder zu.
sentences.l: They squabbled about for a while, but because they were tired, they let it be, and their eyes fell shut again.
sentences.i: They squabbled for a while, but being tired, they let it drop, and their eyes fell shut again.
sentences.n: „zankten sich herum“ (sich herumzanken, ‘to squabble’) is separable. „ließen sies gut sein“ (sies = sie es) is an idiom, ‘they let it be, let it drop.’ „fielen ihnen ... zu“ — dative of possession (ihnen) with zufallen (‘to fall shut’), ‘their eyes fell shut.’

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein fieng sein Spiel von neuem an, suchte den dicksten Stein aus und warf ihn dem ersten Riesen mit aller Gewalt auf die Brust.
sentences.l: The little tailor began its game afresh, picked out the thickest stone, and threw it at the first giant’s breast with all its might.
sentences.i: The little tailor began his game afresh, picked out the biggest stone, and hurled it at the first giant’s chest with all his might.
sentences.n: „fieng ... an“ is the 1857 spelling of fing an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable. „suchte ... aus“ (aussuchen, ‘to pick out’) is likewise separable. „von neuem“ = anew, afresh.

sentences.t: „Das ist zu arg!“ schrie er, sprang wie ein Unsinniger auf und stieß seinen Gesellen wider den Baum daß dieser zitterte.
sentences.l: „This is too much!“ he cried, sprang up like a madman, and shoved his companion against the tree so that it trembled.
sentences.i: „This is too much!“ he cried, sprang up like a madman, and thrust his fellow against the tree so hard that it shook.
sentences.n: „zu arg“ = too much, too bad. „sprang ... auf“ (aufspringen) is separable. „wider“ is an older preposition, ‘against’ (+ accusative). The daß-clause is one of result.

sentences.t: Der andere zahlte mit gleicher Münze, und sie geriethen in solche Wuth, daß sie Bäume ausrissen, auf einander los schlugen, so lang bis sie endlich beide zugleich todt auf die Erde fielen.
sentences.l: The other paid back in the same coin, and they fell into such a rage that they tore up trees, struck out at one another, so long until at last both fell dead to the earth together.
sentences.i: The other paid him back in kind, and they flew into such a rage that they tore up trees and set upon each other, until at last both fell dead to the ground together.
sentences.n: „mit gleicher Münze zahlen“ is an idiom, ‘to pay back in kind.’ „geriethen“ is the 1857 spelling of gerieten (geraten, ‘to fall into [a state]’). „ausrissen“ (ausreißen, ‘to tear out’) and „los schlugen“ (losschlagen, ‘to strike out’) are separable; „zugleich“ = at the same time.

sentences.t: Nun sprang das Schneiderlein herab.
sentences.l: Now the little tailor sprang down.

sentences.t: „Ein Glück nur,“ sprach es, „daß sie den Baum, auf dem ich saß, nicht ausgerissen haben, sonst hätte ich wie ein Eichhörnchen auf einen andern springen müssen: doch unser einer ist flüchtig!“
sentences.l: „A lucky thing only,“ it said, „that they didn’t tear up the tree I sat on, else I’d have had to leap onto another like a squirrel: but the likes of us is nimble!“
sentences.i: „A lucky thing,“ he said, „that they didn’t uproot the tree I was sitting on, or I’d have had to spring onto another like a squirrel — but the likes of me is nimble!“
sentences.n: „hätte ... springen müssen“ — pluperfect subjunctive with a double infinitive, ‘would have had to jump.’ „sonst“ = otherwise, else. „unser einer“ once more, ‘one of us, the likes of us’; „flüchtig“ here = fleet, nimble.

sentences.t: Es zog sein Schwert und versetzte jedem ein paar tüchtige Hiebe in die Brust, dann gieng es hinaus zu den Reitern und sprach „die Arbeit ist gethan, ich habe beiden den Garaus gemacht: aber hart ist es hergegangen, sie haben in der Noth Bäume ausgerissen und sich gewehrt, doch das hilft alles nichts wenn einer kommt wie ich, der siebene auf einen Streich schlägt.“
sentences.l: It drew its sword and dealt each a few hearty blows in the breast, then went out to the horsemen and said, „the work is done, I’ve finished both of them off: but it went hard — in their need they tore up trees and defended themselves, yet all that avails nothing when one comes like me, who strikes seven at one blow.“
sentences.i: He drew his sword and dealt each a few hearty blows to the chest, then went out to the horsemen and said, „The work is done; I’ve finished both of them off. But it was hard going — in their extremity they tore up trees and put up a fight, yet all that’s no use when a man comes along like me, who strikes seven at one blow.“
sentences.n: „versetzte ... Hiebe“ = to deal blows. „gethan“ is the 1857 spelling of getan (thun = tun). „hart ist es hergegangen“ (hergehen) = it went hard, was a rough business. „Noth“ is the 1857 spelling of Not (‘need, distress’), with the old -th-. „sich wehren“ = to defend oneself.

sentences.t: „Seid ihr denn nicht verwundet?“ fragten die Reiter.
sentences.l: „Are you not wounded, then?“ asked the horsemen.
sentences.n: „denn“ is a flavouring particle in questions, ‘then, after all.’

sentences.t: „Das hat gute Wege,“ antwortete der Schneider, „kein Haar haben sie mir gekrümmt.“
sentences.l: „That’s quite all right,“ answered the tailor, „they haven’t bent a hair on me.“
sentences.i: „No fear of that,“ answered the tailor. „They haven’t touched a hair of my head.“
sentences.n: „das hat gute Wege“ is an old idiom, ‘there’s no danger of that, it’s all right.’ „kein Haar krümmen“ = not to harm a hair (of someone); „mir“ is the dative of the person affected.

sentences.t: Die Reiter wollten ihm keinen Glauben beimessen und ritten in den Wald hinein: da fanden sie die Riesen in ihrem Blute schwimmend, und rings herum lagen die ausgerissenen Bäume.
sentences.l: The horsemen would give him no credence and rode into the forest: there they found the giants swimming in their blood, and round about lay the uprooted trees.
sentences.i: The horsemen would not believe him and rode into the forest: there they found the giants weltering in their blood, with the uprooted trees lying all around.
sentences.n: „Glauben beimessen“ = to give credence (+ dative). „ritten ... hinein“ (hineinreiten) is separable. „in ihrem Blute schwimmend“ = swimming/weltering in their blood (Blute has the old dative -e); „rings herum“ = all around.

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein verlangte von dem König die versprochene Belohnung, den aber reute sein Versprechen und er sann aufs neue wie er sich den Helden vom Halse schaffen könnte.
sentences.l: The little tailor demanded of the king the promised reward, but the king rued his promise and pondered anew how he might get the hero off his neck.
sentences.i: The little tailor demanded of the king the promised reward, but the king now regretted his promise, and cast about afresh for how he might rid himself of the hero.
sentences.n: „den aber reute sein Versprechen“ — reuen is impersonal with the person in the accusative (den = the king): ‘his promise rued him,’ i.e. he regretted it. „sich jemanden vom Halse schaffen“ is an idiom, ‘to get someone off one’s back.’ „könnte“ subjunctive of the reported deliberation.

sentences.t: „Ehe du meine Tochter und das halbe Reich erhältst,“ sprach er zu ihm, „mußt du noch eine Heldenthat vollbringen.
sentences.l: „Ere you receive my daughter and half the realm,“ he said to him, „you must yet accomplish one more heroic deed.
sentences.i: „Before you receive my daughter and half the kingdom,“ he said to him, „you must perform one more heroic deed.
sentences.n: „Ehe“ = before (conjunction). „erhältst“ is 2nd sg of erhalten (‘to receive’). „Heldenthat“ is the 1857 spelling of Heldentat (‘heroic deed’), the old -th- again; „vollbringen“ = to accomplish.

sentences.t: In dem Walde läuft ein Einhorn, das großen Schaden anrichtet, das mußt du erst einfangen.“
sentences.l: In the forest there runs a unicorn that does great damage; that you must first catch.“
sentences.i: In the forest there roams a unicorn that does great harm; you must catch it first.“
sentences.n: „anrichtet“ (anrichten, ‘to cause, do [damage]’) and „einfangen“ (‘to catch, capture’) are separable. „das mußt du erst einfangen“ fronts the object das (‘that one you must catch first’).

sentences.t: „Vor einem Einhorne fürchte ich mich noch weniger als vor zwei Riesen; siebene auf einen Streich, das ist meine Sache.“
sentences.l: „Of a unicorn I’m even less afraid than of two giants; seven at one blow — that’s my affair.“
sentences.i: „A unicorn frightens me even less than two giants; seven at one blow — that’s my line of work.“
sentences.n: „sich fürchten vor“ (+ dative) = to be afraid of. „Einhorne“ has the old dative -e. „das ist meine Sache“ = that’s my affair / just my sort of thing.

sentences.t: Er nahm sich einen Strick und eine Axt mit, gieng hinaus in den Wald, und hieß abermals die, welche ihm zugeordnet waren, außen warten.
sentences.l: He took a rope and an axe along with him, went out into the forest, and bade those who were assigned to him wait outside once more.
sentences.i: He took a rope and an axe with him, went out into the forest, and again bade those assigned to him wait outside.
sentences.n: „nahm ... mit“ (mitnehmen) is separable. „abermals“ = once again. „hieß ... warten“ — heißen + bare infinitive, ‘bade (them) wait’; „die, welche ihm zugeordnet waren“ = those who had been assigned to him.

sentences.t: Er brauchte nicht lange zu suchen, das Einhorn kam bald daher, und sprang geradezu auf den Schneider los, als wollte es ihn ohne Umstände aufspießen.
sentences.l: He needed not to seek long; the unicorn soon came along and sprang straight at the tailor, as if it would spear him without ado.
sentences.i: He didn’t have to look long; the unicorn soon came along and sprang straight at the tailor, as if to spear him then and there.
sentences.n: „brauchte nicht zu suchen“ — brauchen + zu = ‘need (not) to.’ „sprang ... los“ (losspringen auf, ‘to spring at’) and „aufspießen“ (‘to impale’) are separable. „ohne Umstände“ = without ado, without ceremony.

sentences.t: „Sachte, sachte,“ sprach er, „so geschwind geht das nicht,“ blieb stehen und wartete bis das Thier ganz nahe war, dann sprang er behendiglich hinter dem Baum.
sentences.l: „Softly, softly,“ he said, „that doesn’t go so fast,“ stood still and waited until the beast was quite near, then sprang nimbly behind the tree.
sentences.i: „Gently, gently,“ he said, „it doesn’t go that quickly,“ stood still and waited until the beast was quite close, then sprang nimbly behind the tree.
sentences.n: „Sachte, sachte“ = gently, easy now. „blieb stehen“ (stehenbleiben) is separable. „Thier“ is the 1857 spelling of Tier (‘animal, beast’), the old -th- again; „behendiglich“ = nimbly (archaic).

sentences.t: Das Einhorn rannte mit aller Kraft gegen den Baum und spießte sein Horn so fest in den Stamm, daß es nicht Kraft genug hatte es wieder heraus zu ziehen, und so war es gefangen.
sentences.l: The unicorn charged with all its might against the tree and drove its horn so fast into the trunk that it hadn’t strength enough to pull it out again, and so it was caught.
sentences.i: The unicorn charged the tree with all its might and drove its horn so fast into the trunk that it hadn’t strength enough to pull it out again, and so it was caught.
sentences.n: „spießte ... in“ = drove/speared into. „heraus zu ziehen“ splits the separable herausziehen (‘to pull out’) around the zu. Two different „es“: the first refers to the unicorn, ‘it had not strength,’ the second is the object, ‘to pull *it* [the horn] out.’

sentences.t: „Jetzt hab ich das Vöglein,“ sagte der Schneider, kam hinter dem Baum hervor, legte dem Einhorn den Strick erst um den Hals, dann hieb er mit der Axt das Horn aus dem Baum und als alles in Ordnung war führte er das Thier ab und brachte es dem König.
sentences.l: „Now I’ve got the little bird,“ said the tailor, came out from behind the tree, laid the rope first about the unicorn’s neck, then with the axe hewed the horn out of the tree, and when all was in order led the beast off and brought it to the king.
sentences.i: „Now I’ve got my little bird,“ said the tailor, came out from behind the tree, first slipped the rope round the unicorn’s neck, then hewed the horn out of the tree with his axe, and when all was in order led the beast off and brought it to the king.
sentences.n: „das Vöglein“ (‘the little bird’) is playful for ‘my prize, my catch.’ „kam ... hervor“ (hervorkommen, ‘to come out’) and „führte ... ab“ (abführen, ‘to lead away’) are separable. „legte dem Einhorn den Strick um den Hals“ — dative of the person/animal, ‘round the unicorn’s neck.’ „hieb“ is the preterite of hauen (‘to hew, strike’).

sentences.t: Der König wollte ihm den verheißenen Lohn noch nicht gewähren, und machte eine dritte Forderung.
sentences.l: The king would not yet grant him the promised reward, and made a third demand.
sentences.i: The king still would not grant him the promised reward, and made a third demand.
sentences.n: „verheißen“ = to promise (elevated); „verheißenen“ is its past participle. „gewähren“ = to grant.

sentences.t: Der Schneider sollte ihm vor der Hochzeit erst ein Wildschwein fangen, das in dem Wald großen Schaden that; die Jäger sollten ihm Beistand leisten.
sentences.l: The tailor was, before the wedding, first to catch him a wild boar that did great damage in the forest; the huntsmen should lend him aid.
sentences.i: Before the wedding, the tailor was first to catch a wild boar that was doing great damage in the forest; the huntsmen were to lend him aid.
sentences.n: „sollte“ / „sollten“ report the king’s stipulation (‘was to’). „that“ is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = tun, ‘to do’). „Beistand leisten“ = to lend aid.

sentences.t: „Gerne,“ sprach der Schneider, „das ist ein Kinderspiel.“
sentences.l: „Gladly,“ said the tailor, „that’s child’s play.“

sentences.t: Die Jäger nahm er nicht mit in den Wald, und sie warens wohl zufrieden, denn das Wildschwein hatte sie schon mehrmals so empfangen daß sie keine Lust hatten ihm nachzustellen.
sentences.l: The huntsmen he did not take with him into the forest, and they were well content with it, for the wild boar had already received them several times in such a way that they had no wish to pursue it.
sentences.i: He did not take the huntsmen into the forest with him, and they were quite content, for the boar had already given them such a reception several times that they had no wish to go after it.
sentences.n: „nahm ... mit“ (mitnehmen) is separable, with the object Die Jäger fronted. „warens“ = waren es, ‘were content with it.’ „nachzustellen“ (nachstellen, ‘to pursue, lie in wait for,’ + dative) is separable, here a zu-infinitive.

sentences.t: Als das Schwein den Schneider erblickte, lief es mit schäumendem Munde und wetzenden Zähnen auf ihn zu, und wollte ihn zur Erde werfen: der flüchtige Held aber sprang in eine Kapelle, die in der Nähe war, und gleich oben zum Fenster in einem Satze wieder hinaus.
sentences.l: When the boar caught sight of the tailor, it ran at him with foaming mouth and gnashing teeth and would have thrown him to the ground: but the nimble hero sprang into a chapel that was nearby, and at once out again, up through the window in one bound.
sentences.i: When the boar caught sight of the tailor, it charged at him with foaming mouth and gnashing teeth, meaning to hurl him to the ground; but the nimble hero sprang into a chapel that was nearby, and at once out again, up through the window in a single leap.
sentences.n: „lief ... auf ihn zu“ (zulaufen auf, ‘to run at’) is separable. „mit schäumendem Munde“ (Munde has the old dative -e) and „wetzenden Zähnen“ are present participles used as adjectives, ‘foaming ... gnashing.’ „flüchtig“ here = fleet, quick to run; „in einem Satze“ = in one leap.

sentences.t: Das Schwein war hinter ihm her gelaufen, er aber hüpfte außen herum und schlug die Thüre hinter ihm zu; da war das wüthende Thier gefangen, das viel zu schwer und unbehilflich war, um zu dem Fenster hinaus zu springen.
sentences.l: The boar had run in after him, but he hopped round outside and slammed the door shut behind it; there the raging beast was caught, being far too heavy and unwieldy to spring out at the window.
sentences.i: The boar had run in after him, but he skipped round the outside and slammed the door shut behind it; and there the raging beast was trapped, being far too heavy and clumsy to leap out through the window.
sentences.n: „hinter ihm her“ = after him, in pursuit. „schlug ... zu“ (zuschlagen, ‘to slam shut’) is separable. „Thüre“ is the 1857 spelling of Türe/Tür; „wüthende“ is the 1857 spelling of wütende (‘raging’). „um ... zu springen“ = ‘too heavy ... *to* leap.’

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein rief die Jäger herbei, die mußten den Gefangenen mit eigenen Augen sehen: der Held aber begab sich zum Könige, der nun, er mochte wollen oder nicht, sein Versprechen halten mußte und ihm seine Tochter und das halbe Königreich übergab.
sentences.l: The little tailor called the huntsmen over; they had to see the captive with their own eyes: but the hero betook himself to the king, who now, whether he would or no, had to keep his promise and handed over to him his daughter and half the kingdom.
sentences.i: The little tailor called the huntsmen over; they had to see the captive with their own eyes; but the hero betook himself to the king, who now, willing or not, had to keep his promise, and handed over to him his daughter and half the kingdom.
sentences.n: „rief ... herbei“ (herbeirufen, ‘to call over’) is separable. „begab sich“ (sich begeben) = betook himself, went; „Könige“ has the old dative -e. „er mochte wollen oder nicht“ = whether he would or not; „übergab“ (übergeben) = handed over.

sentences.t: Hätte er gewußt daß kein Kriegsheld sondern ein Schneiderlein vor ihm stand, es wäre ihm noch mehr zu Herzen gegangen.
sentences.l: Had he known that no war-hero but a little tailor stood before him, it would have gone even more to his heart.
sentences.i: Had he known that it was no war-hero but a little tailor standing before him, it would have grieved him all the more.
sentences.n: „Hätte er gewußt ...“ — a conditional formed by fronting the verb (‘had he known ...’), with subjunctive „wäre ... gegangen“ in the result. „kein ... sondern“ = ‘not a ... but (rather).’ „zu Herzen gehen“ = to go to one’s heart, grieve one.

sentences.t: Die Hochzeit ward also mit großer Pracht und kleiner Freude gehalten, und aus einem Schneider ein König gemacht.
sentences.l: The wedding was thus held with great splendour and little joy, and out of a tailor a king was made.
sentences.i: So the wedding was held with great splendour and little joy, and out of a tailor a king was made.
sentences.n: „ward“ once more — archaic preterite of werden, here passive ‘was held.’ Note the pointed antithesis „großer Pracht und kleiner Freude“ (great splendour, little joy). „aus einem Schneider ein König gemacht“ — [ward] gemacht carried over, ‘a king was made out of a tailor.’

sentences.t: Nach einiger Zeit hörte die junge Königin in der Nacht wie ihr Gemahl im Traume sprach „Junge, mach mir den Wams und flick mir die Hosen, oder ich will dir die Elle über die Ohren schlagen.“
sentences.l: After some time the young queen heard in the night how her husband spoke in his dream, „boy, make me the jerkin and mend me the breeches, or I’ll strike the ell about your ears.“
sentences.i: After some time the young queen heard in the night how her husband talked in his sleep: „Boy, make me the jerkin and patch me the breeches, or I’ll lay the yardstick about your ears.“
sentences.n: „Gemahl“ = spouse, husband (elevated). „im Traume“ (Traume has the old dative -e) = in a dream. „mach mir ... flick mir“ — imperatives with dative mir (‘make/mend me ...’); „die Elle“ is the tailor’s yard-measure — the sleep-talk gives him away as a mere tailor.

sentences.t: Da merkte sie in welcher Gasse der junge Herr geboren war, klagte am andern Morgen ihrem Vater ihr Leid und bat er möchte ihr von dem Manne helfen, der nichts anders als ein Schneider wäre.
sentences.l: Then she perceived in what lane the young lord was born, lamented her sorrow to her father the next morning, and begged that he might help her free of the man, who was nothing other than a tailor.
sentences.i: Then she perceived what lowly lane the young lord had been born in; the next morning she poured out her grief to her father and begged him to rid her of the man, who was nothing but a tailor.
sentences.n: „in welcher Gasse ... geboren“ — being born ‘in a lane’ marks humble origins. „klagte ... ihr Leid“ = to pour out one’s sorrow (+ dative of the person). „bat er möchte ...“ — reported request with subjunctive „möchte ... wäre“; „von dem Manne helfen“ = to help (rid) her of the man.

sentences.t: Der König sprach ihr Trost zu und sagte „laß in der nächsten Nacht deine Schlafkammer offen, meine Diener sollen außen stehen und, wenn er eingeschlafen ist, hineingehen, ihn binden und auf ein Schiff tragen, das ihn in die weite Welt führt.“
sentences.l: The king spoke comfort to her and said, „leave your bedchamber open the next night; my servants shall stand outside and, when he has fallen asleep, go in, bind him, and carry him onto a ship that bears him into the wide world.“
sentences.i: The king spoke comfort to her and said, „Leave your bedchamber open the next night; my servants shall wait outside and, once he has fallen asleep, go in, bind him, and carry him onto a ship that will bear him off into the wide world.“
sentences.n: „sprach ... Trost zu“ (zusprechen, ‘to speak [comfort] to someone’) is separable. „laß ... offen“ = leave open (imperative of lassen). „hineingehen“ (‘to go in’) is separable; „sollen“ governs the string of infinitives (go in, bind, carry).

sentences.t: Die Frau war damit zufrieden, des Königs Waffenträger aber, der alles mit angehört hatte, war dem jungen Herrn gewogen und hinterbrachte ihm den ganzen Anschlag.
sentences.l: The woman was content with it, but the king’s armour-bearer, who had listened in to it all, was well-disposed toward the young lord and secretly reported the whole plot to him.
sentences.i: The wife was content with it, but the king’s armour-bearer, who had overheard it all, was well-disposed toward the young lord and secretly brought word of the whole plot to him.
sentences.n: „damit zufrieden“ = content with it. „mit angehört“ (mit anhören, ‘to listen in, overhear’). „gewogen“ (+ dative) = well-disposed, favourable toward. „hinterbrachte“ (hinterbringen) = to report/convey secretly; „Anschlag“ = plot, scheme.

sentences.t: „Dem Ding will ich einen Riegel vorschieben,“ sagte das Schneiderlein.
sentences.l: „To this business I’ll shoot a bolt,“ said the little tailor.
sentences.i: „I’ll put a stop to that,“ said the little tailor.
sentences.n: „einer Sache einen Riegel vorschieben“ is a set idiom, ‘to put a stop to something, bolt the door on it’; „Dem Ding“ is dative, ‘to the thing/business.’ „vorschieben“ is separable.

sentences.t: Abends legte es sich zu gewöhnlicher Zeit mit seiner Frau zu Bett: als sie glaubte er sei eingeschlafen, stand sie auf, öffnete die Thüre und legte sich wieder.
sentences.l: In the evening it lay down to bed with its wife at the usual time: when she believed he had fallen asleep, she got up, opened the door, and lay down again.
sentences.i: In the evening he went to bed with his wife at the usual hour; when she believed he had fallen asleep, she got up, opened the door, and lay down again.
sentences.n: „zu gewöhnlicher Zeit“ = at the usual time. „sei eingeschlafen“ — subjunctive I in reported belief, ‘had (supposedly) fallen asleep.’ „stand ... auf“ (aufstehen) is separable; „Thüre“ again the 1857 spelling.

sentences.t: Das Schneiderlein, das sich nur stellte als wenn es schlief, fieng an mit heller Stimme zu rufen „Junge, mach mir den Wams und flick mir die Hosen, oder ich will dir die Elle über die Ohren schlagen!
sentences.l: The little tailor, which only pretended as if it slept, began to call with a loud voice, „boy, make me the jerkin and mend me the breeches, or I’ll strike the ell about your ears!
sentences.i: The little tailor, who was only pretending to be asleep, began to call out in a loud voice, „Boy, make me the jerkin and patch me the breeches, or I’ll lay the yardstick about your ears!
sentences.n: „sich stellen als wenn“ = to pretend as if. „fieng an“ (anfangen, 1857 spelling) is separable, ‘began.’ „mit heller Stimme“ = in a loud, clear voice.

sentences.t: ich habe siebene mit einem Streich getroffen, zwei Riesen getödtet, ein Einhorn fortgeführt, und ein Wildschwein gefangen, und sollte mich vor denen fürchten, die draußen vor der Kammer stehen!“
sentences.l: I have struck seven at one blow, killed two giants, led off a unicorn, and caught a wild boar, and should I be afraid of those who stand outside before the chamber!“
sentences.i: I have struck seven at one blow, killed two giants, led off a unicorn, and caught a wild boar — and should I be afraid of those standing outside the chamber!“
sentences.n: A summary boast running through his four exploits. „getödtet“ is the 1857 spelling of getötet. „fortgeführt“ (fortführen, ‘to lead away’). „sollte ich mich ... fürchten“ — the subjunctive of indignant rhetorical question, ‘as if I would fear ...’; „vor denen ... fürchten“ (+ vor + dative).

sentences.t: Als diese den Schneider also sprechen hörten, überkam sie eine große Furcht, sie liefen als wenn das wilde Heer hinter ihnen wäre, und keiner wollte sich mehr an ihn wagen.
sentences.l: When these heard the tailor speak thus, a great fear came over them; they ran as if the Wild Hunt were behind them, and none would venture near him any more.
sentences.i: When they heard the tailor speaking so, a great fear came over them; they fled as if the Wild Hunt were at their heels, and none would venture near him any more.
sentences.n: „also“ = thus, so (not English ‘also’). „überkam sie eine Furcht“ (überkommen) = fear came over them. „das wilde Heer“ is the Wild Hunt, the ghostly host of folklore. „sich an jemanden wagen“ = to dare approach someone.

sentences.t: Also war und blieb das Schneiderlein sein Lebtag ein König.
sentences.l: So the little tailor was and remained a king his life long.
sentences.i: And so the little tailor was, and remained, a king all his life long.
sentences.n: „Also“ closes the tale, ‘so, thus.’ „sein Lebtag“ is an adverbial accusative, ‘all his life long.’
